questions.prompt: Which locator is the most resilient to a redesign that moves elements around the page?
options: An XPath that matches the visible button text 'Sign In'
A locator based on the element's pixel position
questions.explanation: A data-testid is a stable hook added for tests; it survives restyling and relayout. Position-based XPath and visible-text locators break when layout or copy changes.

questions.prompt: Why is Thread.sleep(3000) a poor way to handle a slow-loading element?
options: It either wastes time or still fails when the page is slower than the guess
It only works in headless mode
questions.explanation: A fixed sleep guesses the wait. It is too long on a fast run and too short on a slow one. An explicit WebDriverWait polls for the real condition, so it is both faster and reliable.

options: It can run any number of times, in any order, because it sets up and cleans up its own data
It runs faster than every other test
questions.explanation: An idempotent test owns its data: it creates what it needs and tears it down, so it does not depend on leftovers from another run and passes consistently in any order.

questions.prompt: A test fails intermittently. What is the right first response?
options: Wrap it in an automatic retry and move on
Find and fix the root cause, usually a timing or shared-state bug
questions.explanation: A retry hides the real defect and erodes trust in the suite. Diagnose the timing or state cause and fix it. Retries are a last resort for genuinely external flake, and should always be logged.
